title: The Steel-Drivin' Man: A Tale of John Henry
source: The Steel-Drivin' Man: A Tale of John Henry by Adapted from American folklore and the ballads of the Chesapeake & Ohio Railway

characters.name: John Henry
characters.description: Towering, broad-chested Black steel driver born on a Virginia plantation in the 1840s; calm-eyed, quiet, with skillet-sized hands. After Emancipation he follows the railroad camps, becomes the legendary hammer-swinger at Big Bend Tunnel.
characters.arc: From prophesied infant to legendary steel driver who beats the steam drill and dies at the moment of his triumph.

characters.name: Polly Ann
characters.description: John Henry's small, fierce wife with a yellow kerchief, a laugh that fills clearings and a temper that empties them. Mother, singer, and steel driver in her own right.
characters.arc: Meets John Henry in a Carolina brush-arbor church, becomes his steady partner, takes up his hammer when he's sick, and raises their son alone after his death.

characters.name: The midwife
characters.description: Wipes her hands on her apron in the small cabin where John Henry is born.
characters.arc: Brief presence at the legend's beginning.

characters.name: John Henry's mother
characters.description: An enslaved woman who has known the long road between Virginia plantations; holds the newborn legend in her arms by firelight.
characters.arc: Witnesses the prophecy from her infant son's lips.

scenes.summary: On a black night in the 1840s, John Henry is born in a low Virginia cabin. Broad-chested, old-eyed, the infant reaches up and — so legend says — prophesies that a hammer will be the death of him.

dialogue_excerpts.line: A hammer'll be the death of me.

elements.text: A single lantern burns inside a low Virginia cabin. Rain ticks the tin roof. Shadows pool in every corner.
They say he came on the blackest night the county ever saw — a night so dark the stars themselves stepped back to watch.
The midwife bends over a straw mattress, sleeves rolled. John Henry's mother grips the bedpost, sweat-slick, jaw set.
A newborn's cry splits the room. The midwife lifts the infant — broad-chested, impossibly large, eyes already open and ancient.
Lord. This child come into the world full-grown.
The baby's tiny fist rises toward the rafters, fingers closing on empty air as if gripping an unseen handle.
A hammer... gonna be the death of me.
The mother stares at her son. The lantern flickers. Outside, thunder rolls like distant steel on stone.

scenes.location: Cotton fields, sawmills, and the road to the railroad camps
scenes.summary: John Henry grows like an unshaded hickory — lifting cider barrels at twelve, outworking grown men at sixteen. After Emancipation he chooses the railroad camps for their dollar-and-a-quarter days, ignoring the inner voice that warns him to stop.

elements.text: Sun bakes a cotton field. A boy of twelve hoists a cider barrel onto his shoulder, grinning at slack-jawed field hands.
He grew like an unshaded hickory — straight, hard, reaching for sky no one had promised him.
Years strip away. At sixteen, John Henry swings an axe at a sawmill, splitting timber two grown men couldn't budge.
Step aside, uncle. Let a working man work.
Emancipation. A crowd of freedmen scatters down a dirt road. John Henry stands at a crossroads, listening.
Something inside me said stop, boy. Rest. But a dollar and a quarter a day don't whisper — it hollers.
He shoulders a battered satchel and turns toward distant smoke — the railroad camps rising on the horizon.
Hammer's waitin' on me. I ain't gonna keep it waitin' long.

scenes.location: Brush-arbor church and red dirt road, the Carolinas
scenes.summary: In a pine-board church smelling of turpentine, John Henry's deep voice answers Polly Ann's singing. He walks her home along a red dirt road in near-silence; by her gate, she has already decided to marry him.

elements.text: Sunlight knifes through pine-board slats of a brush-arbor church. Turpentine and sweat in the air. The congregation sways.
Polly Ann stands in a yellow kerchief, small and straight-backed, her voice climbing a hymn into the rafters.
From the back row, John Henry rises — enormous, broad as a doorframe. His bass answers her line, low as river stone.
Their voices braid. Polly Ann's eyes find his across the pews. She does not look away.
EXT. RED DIRT ROAD - LATER. They walk side by side. Pine shadows long. Neither speaks. Her hand brushes his once.
At a weathered gate, Polly Ann stops. Studies him a long moment, like measuring timber.
You sing like a man who means to stay.
I mean to, Polly Ann.
She nods once — the decision already made — and slips through the gate without looking back.
